artists.name: Couple Two T'ree
artists.info:
How  would  you  describe  your  show?We're  both  pretty  deep  in  soul  and  funk  45s  so  that  tends  to  make  up  the  majority  of  our  shows,  but  we  also  collect  other  genres:  Latin,  Jamaican,  garage  rock,  psych,  city  pop,  etc.  We  both  have  a  similar  sensibility  towards  record  collecting  in  terms  of  what  we  listen  for,  the  focus  being  on  obscure  sounds. 
 If  you’re  in  a  collective,  how  did  you  meet  each  other? 
 I  (Joe)  have  been  playing  in  punk  and  hardcore  bands  since  I  was  14,  and  I'vebeen  collecting  records  for  just  as  long.  My  taste  grew  as  a  record  collector/enthusiast  out  a  desire  to  be  a  better,  well  informed  musician.  I  still  play  in  a  hardcore  band,  The  Lion's  Cage,  and  even  though  the  music  I  createis  pretty  different  than  what  I  buy,  collect  and  DJ  the  two  play  a  large  role  in  informing  each  other. 
 If  you  have  guests,  how  do  you  find  and  determine  guests  for  your  show?  There's  a  decent  amount  of  45  collectors  living  in  NY,  people  I've  been  good  friends  over  the  years  through  the  45  scene.  I  often  ask  those  friend's  to  fill  in  on  the  show,  people  I  know  who  have  a  similar  style  to  Couple  Two  T'ree.  Each  of  those  collectors  has  a  slightly  different  sound  in  what  they  look  for  in  45s,  if  you  listen  carefully  to  can  start  to  pick  out  a  DJ's  "sound."  Often  we  end  up  attributing  45s  to  that  DJ,  like  "Oh,  this  sounds  like  a  Joe  45"  because  it  fits  pretty  well  into  my  individual  sensibility.  This  leaves  room  for  guests  to  come  on  the  show  that  fit  the  format  but  also  bring  something  new  and  interesting  to  the  table. 
 How  does  the  Lot  feel  different  than  other  radio  stations? 
 The  Lot  is  different  in  so  many  ways  but  primarily  I'd  say  the  diversity  of  DJs  and  the  emphasis  on  passion.  We  were  one  of  the  earliest  regular  shows  on  the  station  and  I  remember  Francois  giving  us  some  praise  for  our  apparent  passion  for  what  we  do.  That  always  stuck  with  me  and  I've  realized  that's  magic  potion  for  what  has  made  The  Lot  so  amazing.  Every  single  DJ,  guest  or  regular,  that  blesses  that  shipping  container  is  abundantly  passionate  about  music.  That's  what  brought  Alston  and  I  together  as  friends,  our  mutual  passion  for  collecting  45s  and  DJing  and  that's  what  drives  us  to  do  this.show 
 In  what  ways  has  your  involvement  with  the  Lot  changed  how  you  think  about  music  today? 
 The  Lot  has  facilitated  a  super  solid  community  of  DJs  and  collectors  and  through  that  I've  met  some  incredible  people.  That  sense  of  community  has  made  me  even  more  stoked  to  be  doing  the  show  and  sharing  my  collection.  On  the  flip  The  Lot  has  also  opened  my  mind  to  some  new  stuff  that  I  was  never  into,  house  music  specifically.  Recently  started  going  down  that  hole  and  that's  something  I  would  have  never  saw  myself  getting  into  a  few  years  ago. 
 Are  you  connected  with  your  listeners?  If  so,  how? 
 Every  Sunday  there's  the  same  handful  of  listeners  active  in  the  chatroom,  shout  out  Biker  Bob,  Jorg,  Dud,  Toast,  NIC!  It's  pretty  incredible  to  have  real  time  interaction  with  listeners,  to  share  information  about  the  records  and  just  shoot  the  shit.  It's  actually  one  of  my  favorite  things  about  doing  the  show. 
 How  has  the  art  of  DJing  changed  with  the  rise  of  streaming  services? 
 For  me  personally  the  cameras  were  the  big  variable  in  terms  of  live  stream  DJing.  For  a  few  reasons.  I've  never  really  considered  myself  a  DJ,  I'm  not  very  good  from  a  technical  perspective,  but  since  the  inception  of  CTT  I've  made  a  point  to  get  better.  Knowing  that  people  are  listening  and  watching  itcan  be  kind  of  embarrassing  if  you  mess  up.  Second,  there's  a  little  bit  of  a  pressure  to  put  on  a  performance.  It's  not  always  the  most  interesting  to  just  watch  two  dudes  fuck  around  and  nerd  out  with  some  45s  on  a  live  stream,  but  if  those  two  dudes  are  clapping  and  dancing  and  genuinely  having  a  good  time  it's  a  different  story.  Alston  and  I  are  both  super  animate  when  we  DJ  out,  and  the  same  goes  for  when  were  on  air.  I  want  people  to  see  us  on  the  live  stream  and  go  "those  fucking  dudes  love  what  their  doing." 
 How  do  you  see  The  Lot  evolving? 
 It's  hard  to  picture  where  The  Lot  will  be  in  a  year,  or  two,  or  three.  When  it  first  started  it  was  pretty  bare  bones  and  reflecting  on  how  far  it  has  come  in  the  past  two  years  its  unbelievable.  We  recently  had  our  two  year  anniversary  party  at  Brooklyn  Bazaar.  That  night  was  the  most  transformative  night  I've  ever  had  as  a  DJ,  a  collector,  a  musician,  and  a  music  lover.  There  were  so  many  people  are  that  party  that  you  could  barely  walk  from  room  to  room  the  whole  night.  It's  a  difficult  feeling  to  describeIt's  hard  to  picture  where  The  Lot  will  be  in  a  year,  or  two,  or  three.  When  it  first  started  it  was  pretty  bare  bones  and  reflecting  on  how  far  it  has  come  in  the  past  two  years  its  unbelievable.  We  recently  had  our  two  year  anniversary  party  at  Brooklyn  Bazaar.  That  night  was  the  most  transformative  night  I've  ever  had  as  a  DJ,  a  collector,  a  musician,  and  a  music  lover.  There  were  so  many  people  are  that  party  that  you  could  barely  walk  from  room  to  room  the  whole  night.  It's  a  difficult  feeling  to  describe but that  set  we  DJed  at  the  party  was  the  most  important  thing  I've  ever  done  as  a  DJ,  I  felt  legitimately  part  of  something  beautiful  and  important  in  a  way  I  have  never  before.  It  was  a  truly  affirming  and  positive  experience.  So  after  that  it's  hard  to  picture  where  the  station  will  go  in  the  next  few  years,  but  Francois  is  a  genius  and  all  the  people  involved  in  making  The  Lot  happen  are  so  driven  and  passionate  to  spread  the  message.  Wherever  it's  going  from  here  is  going  to  be  tremendous.

artists.name: Camille Vourzay
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

It’s the reflection of what inspires me. Travels, people. Mostly funk music, disco, soul and rock n roll. 
 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 
 I run a record label called Beef Wellington records and I work at a record shop in the city and I book shows. My daily life informs my show as soon as I arrive at work! 
 Name: Camille VourzayShow Name: Camille BWRHometown: Clermont­Ferrand, France; Paris, France. Years of DJing: 5 yearsHow would you describe your show? It’s the reflection of what inspires me. Travels, people. Mostly funk music, disco, soul and rock n roll.What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? I run a record label called Beef Wellington records and I work at a record shop in the city and I book shows. My daily life informs my show as soon as I arrive at work!If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 
 Most of the time they are friends and/or coworkers or DJs that I met at their gig and I invited them. 
 Name: Camille VourzayShow Name: Camille BWRHometown: Clermont­Ferrand, France; Paris, France. Years of DJing: 5 yearsHow would you describe your show? It’s the reflection of what inspires me. Travels, people. Mostly funk music, disco, soul and rock n roll.What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? I run a record label called Beef Wellington records and I work at a record shop in the city and I book shows. My daily life informs my show as soon as I arrive at work!If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show?Most of the time they are friends and/or coworkers or DJs that I met at their gig and I invited them.How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 
 It’s different because it mixes a really large spectrum of people but you still feel it’s the same big family in a way. 
 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 
 It didn’t change my involvement, but it pushes me to have new records almost each time and be better (at least trying to!) 
 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 
 A bit yes, by the different social networks and sometimes I meet them during a set. 
 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 
 It’s the contradiction spirit. The more the streaming services rise, the more I’m gonna tryto find some tunes that they are not on those streaming services!

artists.name: Charlotte von Kotze
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 Brooklyn English is obviously a pun full of irony celebrating accents. It came up as I was discussing about my accent and cultural identities with friends. More importantly, the show is all about an eclectic mix of tracks from traditional and contemporary music/musicians (known, emerging or unknown). The goal is to break categorizations and borders with different styles from the 6 continents; i have also really felt that need after the last presidential elections. I usually start more downtempo/instrumental/ambient, I then move to more traditional and percussive music: recently from Japan and Africa; I'll usually continue the BPM increase through music from Central , Brazil, South America and at 11, I'll either have a guest or go more international house with a touch of afrobeat, afrohouse here and there and towards the end, we'll finish more with jazz or some mid-downtempo new jams from new artists who recently sent me their music (recently from Australia and Iceland). The exciting thing is that i have so many more bands and countries to  research and feature! 

 Occasionally, I will have my friend Erica Roden joining the show (she co-owns the Bed-Stuy record store The Mixtape Shop) but I also have guests who carry an eclectic and international story such as Malcolm Mooney (CAN), Composer Chassol, Jason Lindner ( Now vs Now, collaborations with Donny McCaslin, David Bowie, Mulatu Astatke), Kurt Uenala (for his own project and his current /  past collabs with Depeche Mode) Sam Valenti (Ghostly International founder), Hat (Remix-Culture), Congolese Artist Pierre Kwenders (Moonshine Montreal, Trumpetist Takuya Nakamura, and Bastard Jazz (Lord Echo, Illa J). On the side, I have also taken my show to the next level when I curated a concert featuring Vincent Moon, Priscilla Telmon, Laraaji and a premiere about an Alice Coltrane documentary with The Lot at the San Damiano Mission. We are trying to do more of these in 2018. What’s your relationship with music? 

 (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? Really since I was born: I am initially classically trained (clarinet), I then evolved to a more jazz background with my family who also plays music and eventually started working within the music industry for the last 10 years: as a project manager in the export of international music, then as an indie promoter and for Giant Step, and for the last 4 years, I have been doing Music Supervision. On the side, I curate shows here and there and slowly start learning new instruments as the creation bug is contagious;. 

 How does the daily life inform my show? 

The show is featuring music I discover on the day-to-day, a lot by word of mouth from friends and artists I contacted around the world, but also through exhibitions, books, actively or randomly digging (I recently found a  rare  old 45 vinyl of an anonymous Egyptian artist while I was on fruit market in Paris) and of course travels: Every time I travel (recently in Cuba, France, Germany, Portugal and in a few months, Ghana), I'm trying to meet with the locals to better understand the traditional music scene and culture, that's also the best way to have an authentic travel experience.  

If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

I find and determine the guests the same way you dj: by trusting my gut! It's a lot word of mouth, by following the shows happening in New York so I can offer a platform to them if they are available. I also build topics here and there depending on the season. Bottom line, these guests are echoing eclecticism: they are/were part of big projects and/or have their own projects, they educate us and have a connexion with culture, identity, the world, and history and i try to give them a voice as there is so much we can musically (and also on the human level) learn from them. 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 You do have internet radios but The Lot is the only radio I know who was built thanks to a collaboration with friars / the San Damiano Mission. Also The Lot is one of the rare radios which has a physical community. Paris has Le Mellotron set up at a bar but The Lot built its own cafe, bar, garden. community container and an identity; so no wonder that when they do an event like the 4th of July party, more than 1,000 people are joining. It's only been 2 years but they have achieved so much despite a small team and it's just the beginning. Francois trusted his gut, worked crazy hard with his team and he can be so proud of  him and them. People meet there to catch up with friends but also to exchange about their projects.The Lot does invite big DJs and renown artists but they also trust the voice and tastes of young curators as long as they have ideas and dedications; they really give a voice to many and diversity which result in more education and discoveries for an audience who won't let their choices being dictated by mainstream radios. I want to take this opportunity to deeply thank San Damiano, the whole team especially Francois and Pauline who work days and night and a lot behind the curtain; they are crucial to the project and New York owe them; also the baristas who are always around with their good mood and their photo art directions. THANK YOU! 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

It has deepened my relationship with music: more active listenings to match melodies, beats, and tones; it pushes me to also understand better the music technicalities of composers, their creative process, but also of the mixer, of switching between vinyl and CDJs, of doing smooth transitions. I also mainly consider myself lucky to have this show with Francois, Pauline, and Chris' help . In return, I can't take everything for granted and have to make sure the audience is excited: it pushes me to be curious, to dig for more music when i travel, it pushes me to get specific guests and unique topics (soon, I'll do a couple of shows about music and cinema, a marriage which fascinates me), and to deeply prepare my shows. It should not just be music I like but a curation for the audience. Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 A lot through the chat during the show (I do have some loyal listeners from Brazil who have been listening since Day 1) but also through socials, I'll get some requests after the show regarding specific tracks, archives or even questions regarding guests. It's always a good feeling when someone comes to you at a concert and tells you that they recognize you from the show and love it. It warms you up to know people care about less obvious styles of music. I thank anybody who tuned/tune/or will tune in and of course the guests. On a funny note, a lot of friends have recently thanked me for brightening up their Sundays when they had to work or clean their houses! 

 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

 It has opened doors to more people to join the art of DJing, not just the ones who have the connexions or the big reputation. People are more connected, there are more ways to feature unknown artist and even remix them. There is more authenticity and curation but also desire to go beyond the borders of the mainstream FM/AM system which is dictated a lot by music priorities from the industries. With streaming, there is more freedom of speech and diversity. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 With bigger community because the radio will extend their activity and collaboration with more events, more shows, more festivals and maybe with more radio. On the curation level, i think they will keep having a mix of emerging hosts and artists as well as renown DJs and musicians with shows being about music and / or with interviews.
artists.years: I have actively been collecting music for the last 8 years. I have been curating music for videos (Music Supervision) for the last 4­5 years and I have been DJing / playing music publicly at The Lot and here and there at some locations in the neighborhood for the last year (Achilles’ Heel, Donna Brooklyn, Project Parlor...etc.) However, I am not a regular professional DJ playing at clubs on the day­to­day.

artists.name: Christine Renee
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 A release, a comedy, a tragedy, a visual and visceral connection to groovie beats that span the space/time continuum. 

 What’s your relationship with music? 

 DJ Sound Artist. 

 In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 The daily has to be present. Music coming from that source of “nowness” is relevant and more powerful. 

 If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

 I love to invite people who don’t play on The Lot, who are not from the NY area and who may not be currently playing out. Anytime I can share the talents of friends with a broader audience, I get excited to do so. This is usually what informs my choice in DJ guests. I’ll also invite visual artists, performers and dancers on to the show. Sometimes the Disco Chicken drops in. 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 The community. I think it’s about that first in some ways, so you have music coming from half of a Greenpoint coffee shop. The holy Coffee Shop has always been central to artistic populations. Of course, this links fellow DJs and musicians to each other, and also to the community, ie., the passerby that wants a cup of brew. Then you add the ability to have an interactive, online and visual connection internationally, and your community increases infinitely. There are a lot of layers of goodness. 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

 Having a weekly radio show forced me to play music that I hadn’t had time to internalize to the extent to which I might have in the past when filling my bag for a club. It was a real gift, because then I took less ownership of the songs I played; they became “for others,” more fluid. 

 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 I’d say so. I love seeing familiar names in the chat room and I have gotten to meet some of the people from there. Also, I feel connected energetically. Even though there isn’t a visual audience you still feel how the listeners are receiving it. 

 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

 Maybe that there are more DJs and more music available now than I’ve ever seen before, and this has helped people find their micro niches. A DJ doesn’t have to play club music these days. So maybe it’s allowed people to become more personal in their selections. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 I see it growing like an oak tree--rooting down more with it’s community and growing up and out to people all over the globe.

artists.name: Cyrus Sarrafha
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 An attempt to provide an informative and pleasant listening experience, showcasing the boundaries of progressive rock and improvised jazz. 

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 An enthusiast and a completist disc jockey. An architect by day. 

 If you’re in a collective, how did you meet each other? 

 Co-founder and lyricist of Stone Float, a collaborative duo project with my college friend, Hosain Paknezhad. www.soundcloud.com/stone-float 

 If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

 Usually there is so much I want to share with listeners that I run out of time for having guests. though I think it'll be great to have guests who are interested and/or have knowledgeable in prog rock. However, I have had my good friend Luis Padron as my guest on The Lot twice. www.soundcloud.com/luis-pardon 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 Beside the fact that The Lot is run by awesome friends, the main reason I love Lot is that they insist on entertaining the idea of giving all kinds of music a chance--regardless of any genre. It has never been an easy task for me to arrange a similar type of program on any other radio show, except LPH Radio (thank you Nic Mercer!) who invited me on his show couple of years ago. www.soundcloud.com/lphnyc/lph-radioep-xxxxiiistone-floats-cyrus-sarrafha 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

 I'm usually in disagreement when someone says "there is not enough good music to listen to these days" or "there is no new good music." In fact, there is so much good music out there--both old and new--that I believe, life is simply too short to waste it by listening to bad music. Now, what is considered "bad music", is purely based on one's taste and their personal experiences, mostly driven by memories and nostalgia. Even our attempts to explore new genres has its roots in what we have learned before. It all boils down to a clear understanding of "how is music actually important to you." It is the degree of importance, of the quality of your listening experience that defines your taste. Do you listen to music when travelling to work on daily basis? And does it truly move you to do something great? Does it bring you to a better understanding of yourself? Are you actively listening, or merely hearing it? The answers to these questions move me to prepare what I consider to deserve more attention, and to share with the listeners. 

 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 It's the music that connects us. 

 How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

 I believe the art of DJing involves having many technical abilities as well as an understanding of how to keep your listener excited. I do lack the first half of this formula. So, personally, I don't think it's fair to call myself a DJ. It will be unfair to those who have been doing this for a long time and have mastered--or even, created--all necessary technical requirements. Do I call myself a DJ, as in a disc jockey? Yes, of course. Choosing to buy music over food and being eager to share that experience with others, have happened on numerous occasions! But can I make a roomful of thousands of people dance? No, of course not. But if you just relax in your favorite chair and have your drink and cigar in hand, I can take you somewhere you may have never been before. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 Keep at it! You guys are doing great!
artists.years: Since April 19th, 2015 at Bossa Nova Civic Club

artists.name: Dave Harrington
artists.info:
How would you describe your show?  

 Totally free form.  I like to think of it as simply playing 2 hours of what I feel like listening to, whatever I’m listening at the time or want to spend some more time with.  But, that being said, I’ve taken to screening old movies on the video stream, so sometimes the film influences what I play, or how I mix and move amongst different music.  I also love the occasional theme show too. Recently I did a show that was all Impulse jazz records from the 50s and 60s. 

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 I’m a multi-instrumentalist, producer, and composer. I play in a band called Darkside, I lead my own groups and collaborate with lottos NYC musicians these days mostly in improvised music settings. So even though I’m surrounded by music all the time, there is something special about my shows on The Lot. I think of it as a time where ALL I’m going to do is listening to music, no interruptions, no business, just pure listening. 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 The Lot is amazing because it has managed to seamlessly integrate itself into the music community in New York while simultaneously growing that community and helping musicians and DJs and show promoters meet and hang.  It’s one of the few places in the city where I know that I will always run into someone I know, and more often than not, meet someone new. 

 In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

 I really believe that music isn’t just a passion or a job, at its best, it is a true community.  This is something I have tried to foster and build in my own way in NYC and The Lot has helped me in that ongoing, never-ending project and also shown me that that ethos is really truly possible. 

 Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

 There are certain regulars who usually pop up in the chat room during my shows (you know who you are!) and they always have really positive stuff to say.  I love how friendly and funny and wild the chatroom can be.  Whereas so much of the internet is so different, I feel like it’s just really special that the most controversial conversations during my show usually revolve around people opinions about Jerry Garcia when I start playing Grateful Dead tunes. 

 How do you see The Lot evolving? 

 Who knows?! But all I can say is that I want to continue to be a part of it as it grows, whatever that looks like.  It’s just too fun!
artists.show_name:
Dave Harrington, though I keep thinking I want to change it to 
“CINEMA!
”
artists.years: 13 years since I briefly had a show on WKCR during the one semester Ispent at Columbia University.

artists.name: DJ Lindsey
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 A mix of soulful jams from the past, present and future.

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) 

      I have worked as a DJ full-time for 10 years. Any job before and during has just been a means to allow me to continue to DJ and afford life in NY. 

 In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 I am listening to music and thinking about music most of the day, either for pleasure or for work. When I'm not doing that I'm singing in the shower or to my kids. It's a constant in my house/life. 

      How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

      It's way more of a community than the other station I did a show on. Everyone's friendly and you feel supported. I used to harass Lloyd and Francois about my stats. "How many people are listening every month?" They used to laugh at me and not answer. I finally relaxed a little, and I really think my show is more interesting because I don't have the pressure that I felt before on my old show on another station. It's just way more natural, way more quality control goes into each show on my part because I can do exactly what I want. I don't feel like I have to please anyone and it can just be for people who enjoy my perspective on music. 

      In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

      I am able to listen to and share way more quality stuff on my show. When I do play out at a bar now, I feel way more pressure than ever to please the crowd. Bars don't really have the ability to nurture a night and let it gradually build like they used to so I really have to bend to the whims of the crowd. I'm not a marquee headlining DJ so people don't necessarily come out waiting to hear what I'm gonna play. A lot of times I get booked because I am good (toot) and local and they know I will show up and be professional. If I was only doing stuff like that, I wouldn't be able to play slower stuff, older stuff, weirder stuff. So doing my show on The Lot is still a lot of fun for me, I get to just scour the internet for new artists/producers, old gems and support the stuff my friends are doing. 

      Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

      I definitely utilize the chat during my show. It's fun to see what folks think of the music in real time and whatever random gifs and conversations pop up. I promote my show on social media and when I have time I send out a tracklist of songs from my previous shows so people know what’s happening and when. 

      How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

      I don't know that streaming services has changed DJing. I saw a big jump around 2010-2011 when everyone started using controllers and digital DJing became the industry standard and more affordable. I'm curious to see what happens when iTunes ends though. I know Serato made an attempt at integrating Pulselocker into their software but not sure how that went. At this point a lot of DJs are just using USB sticks and organizing music at home before a gig. It feels like a lot of folks are in the process of figuring out easier ways to transport and organize music. Having it all there for you could be good if you're able to organize things in your mind on the fly. For some folks like me, having some parameters is better. 

      How do you see The Lot evolving? 

      I just see it getting bigger---big guests dropping in to guest, bigger DJs doing shows, and a bigger community component which is what I see being a priority for the station.

artists.name: Falty DL
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 A mixed bag of whatever I am feeling at the moment with an emphasis on unreleased music from unknown producers. I also use the show as a platform for debuting music I release on my label, Blueberry Records. I run the gamut of bpms from about 70 to 180. Everything is fair game. I try and shy away from 4X4 music as I feel it is my duty to help people find the down beat without it being spelled out for them. There is so much house and techno being dropped everywhere, I often fear folks will forget what rhythm really is. 

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

 Producing music serves many purposes for me, some I’m still figuring out. It’s essential to the core of my being to make music. If I stop, I lose my mind and become very irritable. It’s my pressure release valve and the healthiest habit I’ve ever formed. How does air and water inform your being? 

 If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

 Folks who seem to be real. 

 How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

 It’s completely independent. No outside money. It’s not corrupt or trying to gain anything off the back of others hard work. Its a service for and by the people. Plus, you know, coffee. 

      In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

 I’m more open to collaboration now then ever. Because of The Lot I’ve met many young talented hungry musicians. It’s a second home to me now and simply where I go when I need to talk to other djs about certain aspects of our lifestyles. Most people don’t know this, but djs and producers and a very unique set of obstacles that there really isn’t a clear cut pathway for us to follow. We are literally figuring this shit out as we go. What worked for one, won’t necessarily work for others. 

Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

The chat room and DMs are nice. I try not to listen to feedback much. It taints my vision. And rarely ever improves anything. 

How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services?  

Smile, you’re on TV!  

How do you see The Lot evolving? 

Not sure. And not up to me, thank god. I shouldn’t be in charge of anything. I’d ruin it. But I’m on board, and The Lot has not done a single thing I have not liked yet. So, let’s see...

artists.name: How Can I Help You?
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

A: Loose. It tends to be in that broad ‘dance music’ end of the spectrum, but really it’s all over the place in terms of genres & eras. The three of us like everything, we are such different people, and never manage tell each other what we’re bringing so it always ends up being a surprise. 

C: Our show is very freeform. Miles, Alejandra and I travel a lot so often one or even two of us can’t make the show. I think we all voyage through a pretty diverse range of music. I like to try and switch the vibes and energy up by throwing a curveball in but we met by DJing parties together so ultimately it always ends up in the dance world. 

M: How Can I Help You? Is a show in which the three of us come together to share with each other (and the listener) an eclectic mixture of music from our various adventures in crate digging. We sometimes back to back, and other times play individual sets. It's a lot of fun for us to meet each other once a month in the booth and show each other what has been inspiring us lately. Playing together makes for a very productive challenge, to expand our perspectives as music lovers and disc jockeys, and find coherence in mixing music from a very wide range of cultural and temporal origins. 

What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

A: Music has for some reason been what I’ve revolved my life around since I was a kid. Hunting for music has always been a hobby, but currently I DJ regularly a few times a week so I tend to look for new stuff more frequently. The radio is also a fun outlet to play things I’m into but can’t play out. 

C: I was really into music in high school and I had a bunch of friends who were pushing me to DJ. I was always a bit of an outsider and I tended to alienate myself. Growing up in NYC all my friends listened to Rap. They were all die hard Cam’ron fans, he was the superstar and to this day I am super fond of Camron partly because of that. But I found myself listening to other styles of music outside of my direct social scene and I wasn’t sure how that would come across. 

M: I have made music longer than I have DJ'd. Pow Pow Family Band is the name of the project for which I write songs. This is a part of my life that means a lot to me, but it is mostly focused on lyrical expression. Djing for me is a wonderful opportunity to explore and share the sonic and rhythmic aspects of  music that I love. It is less personal than writing a song, but therefore a lot more fun. Singing a song feels at times like therapy... but djing other people's music is curation, and there is something quite wonderful about taking the music that you love and sharing it with the people around you, it feels like holding up a painting and saying "hey guys... isn't this nice?" 

If you’re in a collective, how did you meet each other?  

M: Cole and I met in High School, Alejandra and I met when we were booked to DJ at the same venue. That being said the Lot certainly brought us closer together. We began by guesting on each other's slots in the early days of the station, and soon realized that we wanted to play together as often as possible. 

C: Miles and I were friends in high school, we used to hang out and listen to music after school. He introduced me to Ale a year or two ago because he thought we would get along. We did. 

A: I met Miles at a hotel we were both DJing at and he introduced me to Cole. We ended up playing a party together and became good friends after that. I’m not sure I’d say we’re a collective, we each used to do shows separately on the Lot but since we were DJing and hanging it out so often it was way more fun to do a show together. 

How does the Lot Feel different than other radio stations? 

M: When I played my first show on the Lot, it was quite early in the life of the station. I asked Francois if there was any direction he would prefer that I take my show, if there was a particular genre or theme that the station identified with. He seemed surprised by the question and said something along the lines of: "We just want DJs to play what they want to play." I think this is exactly what is so wonderful about the Lot. In addition to that, there is a real sense of community and collaboration at the Lot, where I feel like most musical environments these days are plagued by competitive attitudes, the lot is a place where people really appreciate and support each other. 

C: I love how open the Lot is with its programing. The station is so confident in the local scene; it has put faith in the community and I think it has really paid off. 

A: In New York nothing has a similar sense of community. That’s my favorite thing about it. 

In what ways has your involvement with the Lot changed how you think about music today? 

M: I feel like it has encouraged me to do better in the way that I search for music. I have a lot of respect for the community of dj's and listeners, and so I want every show to have something interesting and new. I think this self imposed challenge has expanded my horizons dramatically since I became involved.  

A: Again, it’s given me a community through music. When I moved to New York I was struggling for a bit to find my people until I started doing the radio shows. 

Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

C: Often friends tune in. Shout out to them. I have met some of the chat regulars at the parties over the years. Insanely sweet humans, shout out to them they really have created such a positive and troll free chat room. 

How has the art of Doing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

M: I think that to play on the radio focuses the DJ on selection instead of crowd control. I feel like it perhaps reinforces the culture of crate digging which is think is a wonderful thing. 

C: Much like any media in the digital age, consumption and distribution is happening at a massive level. I think skateboarding is a great example of the evolution of “content.” Skaters are posting to instagram at a blistering pace when back in the day major companies used to put out a video every couple years or so. With mixcloud and soundcloud thousands of mixes are being released every day. It can be overwhelming but honestly I think it’s a great thing. 

A: It’s made DJing more accessible and finding music easier, but for me DJing remains a live, dance floor thing.  

How do you see The Lot evolving? 

M: It has already become such a fulcrum of New York's music culture. I don't know exactly where it will go from here, but I know that it will continue to bring people together. 

A: Many of the events they have produced have been really special so it’ll be exciting to see what events the future brings.
artists.real_name: Alejandra  Sabillón,  Cole  Evelev,  Miles  Robbins
artists.hometown: San  Pedro  Sula,  Honduras  /  New  York  /  New  York.

artists.name: Justin Strauss
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

 A mix of many types of music I love. 

 What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

Music has been my life. I started playing in a band, became a DJ, produce and remix records. 

If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

I have lots of DJ friends and whenever they are in town I ask if they’d like to come on The Lot Radio with me. They never say no. 

How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

It’s very special and feels like a community and has connected people together in a way I’ve not seen in this town in a long while. 

In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? /n/nHasn’t changed how I think about music, but has exposed me to some things I otherwise might not have heard. 

I love the chat room on The Lot Radio. The listeners are very involved and responsive and I am the same. 

It just makes it easier I guess for people to listen and find music they like. I don’t know if that makes it better or worse but it’s the way many people get their music these days. /n/nIt’s always looking for new ways to share music and community whether through its concerts at the church across the street or the special parties they do. The Lot Radio is always looking to doing something special. /n/n

artists.name: Kamron Saniee
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

Timeless, transcendent statements of the human capacity for writing great music! Well, the title I chose describes a wide range of music, while emphasizing certain threads: the inseparable influence of traditional music on classical repertoire, in non-western as well as western music, and the role of systematic techniques and machines in expanding the possibilities of composition. There is also a focus on works whose composition draws from classical systems; which employ the western classical language to express the traditional music of various cultures anew; on recordings which highlight (and cross) notions of ’national styles’; and also on interpretations of this music which I find compelling – the performances which manage to speak directly to your inner being. 

 What’s your relationship to music? 

I’ve been a musician all my life, starting with classical violin studies from an early age, which led to studying with master teachers and to spending an important part of my adolescence in the practice room. It took me a few years after ending my classical training to understand that background, both musically and non-musically. I became an electronic musician in order to contribute to what I felt to be the musical expressions of my time. But I’m not so concerned with the ‘new' so much as with sharing music which moves people in a fundamental way. In that sense, it’s been a natural thing to do the radioshow in parallel to my electronic practice, which is motivated by the same principle. 

If you’re in a collective, how did you meet each other? 

The SVS Records extended community is probably the closest thing to a collective which I’m a part of. During their summer residencies, people share knowledge about production and composition, collaborate around the clock, cook for one other, etc. I knew a member (Max Lange) who invited me to join their residency at the 4DSound Spatial Sound Institute in Budapest, since I also make spatial music, and the experience turned out to have some surprising parallels to the classical festivals I took part in as a teenager. 

If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

In the first year of the show, many guests were musician friends or collaborators who write music informed by classical repertoire, studied performance, or have a perspective on this culture which informs another artistic project. More recently I’ve started to invite guests on whom I don’t know personally, but think would be a good fit. One repeat guest has been Gordon Minette, with whom I studied composition some years ago. The second time he came on, I had chosen pieces by Lekeu and Chausson, and he by Fauré, Koechlin, and Boulanger. This uncoordinated ‘Franco-Belgian episode’ was a highlight which I’ve chosen to share as a favorite archived show. 

How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

To locals, I’d say it has more of a physical presence than other stations, which leads to an important community aspect. Aside from being located in a culturally active part of town, locals can easily join listeners seated outside the station, and those attending church services at the Mission across the street often find out and attend concerts hosted by The Lot. 

In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

Preparing for this radio show has certainly changed the way I listen to many familiar pieces. But more significantly, I’ve discovered some truly amazing music for the first time. I realized on a new level how the tidal waves in this ocean will just keep coming. In other words, it’s been a humbling experience to prepare material for these shows. This quote of Guillaume Lekeu, a young and brilliant composer who died at age 24, summarizes this feeling : “Le vieux J.-S. Bach en était arrivé à écrire des mélodies de telle longueur qu'un seul exposé suffisait […] à parfaire un morceau de musique” (“Bach was able to write melodies so long, that a single instance of one constitutes a perfect piece of music”) – there’s both great humility and a dialogue across generations there. One can not be reminded enough that the classical tradition has cultivated so much transcendent music, and that through this tradition, we are still listening to and communicating intimately with people who’ve been gone for half a millennium. 

Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

The radio show started with The Lot’s classical New Years concert in 2017, which I helped organize and performed at the violin with pianist Eduard Laurel. On a few occasions this year, people who attended the concert or who tune in to the radio show approached me in public, something which always counts as a special connection with a listener. Other times the chatroom, social media, and The Lot courtyard serve as a forum to talk about the music. 

How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

Streaming services, and the playlists generated for them, are a convenient form of music-sharing online. As a result people might have less time or bandwidth for mixes these days, but the effect on the art and craft of DJing is second-order. As long as people change, so will styles of playing music. The function of a mix remains the same, but the art may evolve to satisfy that function. 

Through the musicians who are part of the extended community, and by making adventurous events happen. For one thing, I can anticipate more classically-oriented concerts, but there is also a cultural exchange with contemporary music from the rest of the world which can find new audiences in New York through The Lot. As long as it remains a project with inclusive and broad appeal, it will continue to evolve.
artists.show_name: Radioshow for Worldwide Classical, Traditional and Systematic Music

artists.name: Kristin Malossi / Brandon Wilner
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

K: Our show is live on Wednesdays from 8am - 10am. I always try to enter each show firmly rooted in that time frame, but also aware that there are listeners abroad who are much further along in their day than we are. I think we feel compelled to play a little bit of everything as a reflection of our tastes. We'll play ambient records, jazz, r&b/acid jazz, reggae/lovers rock/dub, experimental electronics, funk, electro, house & techno, etc. We cover a lot of ground in those two hours. Nothing is off-limits. 

B: The show description used on our show's calendar entry says that we think that "funk and soul are not simply genres, but are rather impartial qualities emitted when humans need them, and that they are to be found across music writ large." I still think this idea governs much of what Kristin and I will bring for each show, as well as my music taste in general. I start with genres (like reggae, jazz, soul, disco, house) that have historically bled into and influenced each other, and I try to work with and show those connections across the two hours of the show.  

What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

K: Music has been my long-term lifeline, so to me DJing, selecting, & sharing makes quite a bit of sense. I came into my DJ identity as a lover of the dance floor but need all kinds of music in my life. I am constantly looking for music to play on the radio show. I am also constantly looking for music to play in my DJ sets at clubs. I love finding songs that at first I believe are more appropriate for one of those settings over the other, and then realizing that they work well in either place. 

B: I work for the music website Resident Advisor, but I wouldn't say that the job is entirely representative of my relationship to music. I've been interested in music for as long as I can remember, and I've come to think of music as my favorite episteme—a direct transfer of feelings or ideas, which can help the listener to understand the personal or historical conditions in which it was made. Music is also what governs most of my interactions with New York City and the people who live here. I'm pretty constantly visiting the city's record shops or going out to hear music. Every time I set foot in any of these places, I leave with someone influence that will be audible in the show. 

If you’re in a collective, how did you meet each other? 

 K: Brandon & I became acquainted through a music sharing group on Facebook. We met on the dance floor at a friend's party, introduced by mutual friends. 

B: Kristin and I met through a secret Facebook group for music heads. It has a small enough membership that you can get to know people's tastes and personalities through their posts and interactions within the group. I moved from Chicago to New York after having been in that group for a couple of years. I met her at a party and already had a sense of who she was. We were starting to hang out and play music together right around when The Lot was starting up, so it made sense to dive into the show together. 

If you have guests, how do you find and determine guests for your show? 

B: We don't often have guests on our show. It's more our time to get together and share what we've been enjoying in the two-week interval between shows. When we need to find a substitute for our show, we'll just ask one of our many talented friends whose taste we admire and who we think can add some verve to the platform. 

How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

K: It's quite a feat to bring nearly all music scenes of a city as big as New York together in one studio, but somehow The Lot does it. Perhaps this is a staple of plenty of online radio at this point, but you really never know what you're going to get when you tune into The Lot. It's also in my experience the most social radio station I've ever been to, which really enhances this community-merging aspect. 

B: The most striking difference between The Lot and other stations I've contributed to is that it has this magnificent outward-facing aspect, the cafe and seating area. Radio stations are generally quite insular, so you don't really get to know many people there other than the hosts whose shows come before or after yours. I wind up going to The Lot all the time outside of when I'm playing there. New York's music scene can tend to self-segregate based on taste—techno people go to the techno parties, disco people go to disco parties. The station provides a commons for mingling and hearing what others are up to. 

In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

K: I think it's a necessary privilege for DJs to have radio shows these days. For me, our show is where I get to sketch out ideas, try them out in public for the first time. The outcome matters because you do have an audience, so you want it to be good, but because the format is radio, it's slightly more inconsequential and you can take risks that would maybe be a bit scarier in a club. As I mentioned above, I'm always finding songs that I wouldn't necessarily consider for the dance floor, and then find that they do in fact work in unexpected ways. I'm extremely indebted to The Lot - it's definitely made me a better DJ! 

B: Doing a regular radio show can pretty easily make you insecure about your record collection. I don't want to feel like I'm just repeating myself or phoning it in. So it's certainly ramped up what was already a steady listening habit. I'd hope that there is a commensurate learning curve too. Any research comes more easily when there's an immediate reason to be doing it. A radio show is a perfect avenue for sustained research. 

Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

K: We definitely participate in the chat during our show - I find it hard not to! A few listeners have become loyal followers on social media, and we've even met on person who we only knew through the chat at a recent bbq - shoutout Toast! 

B: Besides friends of mine who tune in, I'm just connected with listeners through The Lot's chatroom during the show each week. I've started to learn somethings about the chat regulars, and vice versa. Our show is in the morning, and we always eat a banana or apple for breakfast. This has somehow become a ceremonious thing and a regular topic of conversation in the chat. 

How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

 K: I don't think it's really changed the art of djing at all, to be honest! Although it certainly is changing the way the community at large consumes music. I understand now that certain music is more accessible via streaming, that the biggest streaming platforms curate a listener's taste for them (or will eventually) but DJs are (supposed to be) diggers, they're supposed to find their own sound and sift through the rest, to push back against the algorithm. Maybe the biggest impact streaming will have on DJs is when our favorite artists stop making music because they can't afford to do so anytime soon. 

 B: I think that streaming services just help us to realize and appreciate how much music is out there. The only one I really use is YouTube, and when my listening habits drifted to that platform around 2008 or 2009, I felt like I could better visualize just how little I know, even when thinking about a pretty specific genre or sub-genre. There's already been plenty of recent conjecture about this kind of availability and the rising importance of a DJ or "curatorial" voice. As a person who DJs, I just think that it's helped me take the practice a lot more seriously. I always know that I've got more work to do, and it's a tool I use to broaden my intuition continually. As for audio-visual streams like The Lot, I think that these have helped provide an insight into what DJing is for those who don't actively seek it out. Whereas just a few years ago you'd have to wind up at a party to hear, see, and understand a DJ's performance, this newer model makes it so that anyone might stumble onto the stream. My parents, for instance, can actually see me touching and mixing records. That hadn't really happened before The Lot. 

How do you see The Lot evolving?  

K: Onward & upward =) 

B: The schedule's certainly tighter and less flexible, and I see more and more big touring names featured all the time. I'm of course excited that it's growing and reaching a wider audience, but I hope its core stays local. New York has so many amazing DJs, as shown by how quickly The Lot caught on. I'd just hope that it continues to advocate and be a place for these people.
artists.show_name: DJ Voices & Unscented DJ (formerly Go Cool with Kristin & Brandon)

artists.name: Love Injection
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

B: All over the place? We truly like to not discriminate in terms of genres or sounds. While we enjoy a lot of overlap in our taste, we have slightly different musical backgrounds: Paul’s is more soulful, NY-sound while I’m a little more into new wave/post punk inspired stuff. It makes for a good balance between us both but also keeps us on our toes. 

B: I always was drawn to music, musicians, DJs and dancers somehow. I was lucky enough to be brought into a community of music lovers that was very nurturing. It was through these friends that I began to play records about 3-4 years ago. I did it as a hobby up until this year, when life threw a curveball and I transitioned to DJing full time. I had booked and managed my friends’ band for a bit, but it wasn’t until early 2017 that I started to learn about writing and playing music through Luke Jenner, who’s become a mentor and bandmate. I find that the music I want to write and produce is more in line with what I get most excited about sharing during the show--something that’s more esoteric and out of leftfield, perhaps. Songs, not tracks. 

P: I interned at Giant Step Records starting out, then worked in the marketing office of one of the last NY mega clubs for almost 10 years. For the last 4 or so, my day job and music life has been separated, which has been nice. Your relationship with music and how you consume it changes when you have a weekly radio show. I don’t sit with music as much as I’d like to anymore, but I’m discovering new and old things at an accelerated pace which is exciting. We produce the Classic Album Sundays NY sessions, so at least once a month we’re sitting down in front of a hifi to listen to an album in its entirety. Wish I had time to do it more often. 

B: We met at Academy Records in Greenpoint a little over 3 years ago in the Disco 12”s section. Paul introduced himself and contacted me online a few days later. We had a lot of mutual friends, but our paths hadn’t crossed yet. He wrote in his message that he was staring a zine (Love Injection) and asked if I wanted to contribute, to which I said yes and suggested we throw a launch party for the release of Issue One. Things took off from there and we became partners in every sense. The zine became the glue, and we started DJing out as a pair more often, eventually combining our lives in many ways — except our record collections (laughs). A few weeks after the birth of The Lot Radio, Francois reached out and asked us if we were free to play a Saturday morning. We were excited to be invited, but a little worried about the time slot. It ended up being so wonderful that we’ve been playing every Saturday 10am-12pm ever since. We hope it never ever ends. 

P: We generally only have guests when we’re out of town. We’ve got plenty of friends with great taste, so it’s never difficult to find someone to fill in. 

B: It filled a void we didn’t even know we had. It’s hyperlocal and has really made an impact in our community. Before The Lot there was little overlap in our scenes and it felt like sub-communities were more segregated. The Lot has given us a physical space to gather around music. It’s incredibly rare and so important, especially given the vanishing culture spaces and places of a largely gentrified NYC. 

B: Having a weekly show means you go through a boatload of music. We try not to repeat stuff we play as much as possible and there’s an enormous amount of good music to explore--so why limit yourself? We definitely are more laser focused in terms of what we seek for radio play specifically, versus music you’d play at a club gig. It’s a different state of mind and in different context altogether, so I think we learned about the psychology of listening and context more than anything else. We also have opened ourselves up to new sounds, regions, and eras that we may not have encountered otherwise. 

P: You can play virtually anything on a morning show, which makes the amount of music you can buy literally limitless, which makes organizing said music virtually impossible. We use it as an opportunity to relearn our collections, and give purpose to the weirder things that don’t always have a place on a dancefloor. 

P: S/o to BikerBob, ChirpChirp, Nicholas, Njorg, Ella, B12, Gay, Toast, and Guest923 for the mind blowing GIFs. Always hanging in the chat and bringing the good vibes. We’ve had the pleasure of meeting some of them during a few of The Lot parties. Bob & Angie sent us a greeting card thanking us for the music and it was the sweetest thing ever. 

B: I think of the immediacy that the Internet and the rise of streaming services has given people the ability to have everything now, and I don’t necessarily think that’s healthy. The role of the DJ is to search for sounds, spend time curating your collection, and presenting it to an audience while telling a story. Not sure if it’s changed the way I DJ, but it has definitely affected the way I come across music sometimes. While I do agree that we are sharing music that ultimately we don’t own, I also think we’ve just grown too used to having it our way: Spotify, YouTube, Apple Music, Shazam, etc. We’ve all seen the blue phone screen at the club. In the ears of the average listener, I feel like music is seen as something that’s quickly consumed--a throwaway product. I’m at times bummed that the average listener is force-fed Top 40 music and how the industry at large is mass producing recycled singles. I suppose it’s why we love hosting Classic Album Sundays so much--the idea of immersing yourself in a full album it’s something we don’t do anymore. We should be able to sit down,, shut up, quit scrolling, and simply listen to albums. The music we love deserves more than us just using it as background noise as we get from point A to B. The artists poured their soul into the work, and we owe it to them to really listen. 

P: I don’t think it’s changed the way I DJ at all. The only thing I can think of is how YouTube algorithms drive views to obscure records, which drives the price on Discogs and ultimately leads to a re-issue of something. Sometimes for the better, sometimes for the worse. 

How do you see The Lot evolving? 

B: I can only hope for more of what we’ve seen so far: more opportunities to celebrate and cross-pollinate ideas and activism. We are living through one of the toughest periods politically, since I moved to the US, and it’s up to us to be the voice of resistance: through music, dancing, creation and unity. It’s also up to us to try to try to sustain the culture and vibrancy of our city at all costs and put forth the ideals of our communities. I think, at the end of the day, we all are part of The Lot community and want to see it thrive no matter what. We’ll be there to do whatever’s needed to make sure we can continue to share this special triangular spot as long as possible. 

P: They’ve evolved so gracefully – I’m excited to see what’s to come. They have a captive audience — we’ve seen them pull 1,000+ people on a moment’s notice for their parties. That’s a lot of power – harnessing it to do social good is something we’ve seen they are capable of and would love to see more of.
artists.hometown: Paul is from New York City; Barbie is from Buenos Aires, Argentina.

artists.name: Maachew Bentley & Nasty Nigel
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

MB: A steady balance of World Music with Deep House, DnB, Jungle, Tech/Rave infusions. 

NN: If we’re really feeling a rap tune, we’ll pepper it in for sure. 

What’s your relationship with music? (i.e. musician, enthusiast, work in the industry, etc.) In general, how does your daily life inform your show? 

MB: We believe our lifestyles as musicians and aspiring producers put us in front of new perspectives and sounds every night whether it be raving or collaborative in-studio experiences. 

NN: It has definitely been a forever evolving experience where growing old with music just seems right. Knowing that I can immerse myself into something completely different in a decade is exciting. 

If you’re in a collective, how did you meet each other? 

MB: We met over a decade ago now in High School through mutual friends in queens and have been hanging out, making music, partying, traveling together since. 

How does The Lot feel different than other radio stations? 

MB: We have the freedom to always experiment! The Lot doesn’t bind us to a sound or a format. 

NN: It really is a community. A 2pm show can turn into an 8pm hang. It’s awesome to see the numbers of listeners, but it’s even tighter meeting them grabbing coffee or hanging by the fire. 

In what ways has your involvement with The Lot changed how you think about music today? 

MB: Best way to put it would be that the very existence of The Lot has reinforced that our opinion that music and new thoughts are infinite; reinforced our belief in community! 

Are you connected with your listeners? If so, how? 

MB: The Lot chatroom is the GOAT (Greatest Of All Time). 

 NN: The Lot chatroom IRL is just as strong too! 

How has the art of DJing changed with the rise of streaming services? 

MB: I don’t think it has on a smaller intimate scale quite yet. I don’t use streaming services for discovery really. 

NN: You’ll get lucky if Shazam picks up a track to add to your Spotify playlist. It’s still “track id?” over here. 

How do you see The Lot evolving? 

MB: Thankfully, broadcasting online has opened the floodgates for new shows around the world. Perhaps adding a new location in another city? Perhaps a venue for the roster to have indoor events/parties not contingent on weather? I’m happy with the with way it stands now to be honest, but those would be cool additions.

artists.name: Max Pask
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

Anything goes. 

I work as a DJ, so I get new (and old) music all the time. What I play on the show is usually the latest new (and old) stuff I've gotten in recent weeks. 

All the people I work with are just old friends really. 

The radio does a fine job at getting special guests, so if I invite someone, it's because they're friends and have a great record collection. 

One great thing about The Lot is that it's deeply rooted in the local community. 

It hasn't changed it much, but I'm grateful for two hours a month of air time where I get to do and play whatever I want. 

Some reach out via social media, but there isn't much more of a connection than that. 

I don't think there's much of a correlation to be honest. 

I hope it keeps reaching new listeners as it grows, but i hope it doesn't change much, it's great the way it is!
artists.years: I sold my drum kit to buy turntables in 1994.

artists.name: Nadine Régné
artists.info:
How would you describe your show? 

DOG BITES GIRL is a rock n roll show centered around the ideologies of Roi. We do not live in the confines of rock n roll though we interpret the rules of rock n roll as there being no rules. One may do whatever they please. I’d say that’s a pretty solid projection of what it sounds like inside my head. 

Music is everything to me, and everything is music, as we try to reflect in our show. 

Roi and i met on February 28, 2013 in Kuta, Lombok in Indonesia when he was six weeks old. We have been inseparable ever since. 

Because our show is a bit more unorthodox than most of the others, we do not limit our guests to straight and proper “DJ’s” and welcome friends whose musical tastes we admire. We’ve only had one show with a person who was intended to be a proper lecturer, but we hope to continue to host minds to discuss issues such as conservation and preservation of nature and what we can all do to help protect our planet and our interspecies neighbors. In terms of our proper DJ guests, I like to show the different sides, the rock n roller backgrounds, that many may not realize as dance music, be it disco or dub, has been more of a focus of their careers they’re known for. We are also working to have more interviews hosted by Richard Alvarez of creators and movers and shakers reflecting on interconnections of different disciplines of art, their histories, progressions of their works, and their plans for the future. 

The Lot is such a special place because of its inclusiveness of everyone and anyone doing something interesting in New York or who happens to be in town. They provide such a unique opportunity in radio that proves limitless, not being regulated by the FCC and having such a diverse and international following it’s built up so quickly. You really have complete freedom on The Lot and I am grateful each show to have that outlet to express things purely and truly how I want to. 

It’s definitely made me recognize how special so many of the sounds I hear around me truly are and how they can be isolated and manipulated to run hand in hand with more conventional forms of music. To me, radio is now such an experimental platform to see how sound affects people and their mindsets. I play birds, rants of the insane on the train, Roi’s growls, and street noises, and I feel it adds context to each show and puts the listener in a more full atmosphere. It’s not something as easily done or as heard of on the dance floor of a club, where you naturally have so many aspects adding to your experience and the atmosphere around you. With radio, you are able to isolate the listener and control the experience you’re feeding them. I’ve also met so many wonderful and new friends through the radio who have opened my mind to how music is played and digested in different realms of the scene. 

Not necessarily because of the internet aspect of things. Though the chat helps, I tend to stay away from it. But yes, the connections I’ve made to my listeners have made me feel a little less insane about how my head works and I appreciate that others appreciate it too! I had no idea how my show would be received and I’m truly happy that others find it entertaining (though some are still confused by it).  

DJing has changed completely with the rise of technology in general, it is a far more accessible activity now. Streaming services definitely add to that. Anyone can do it now, anyone can broadcast out to the world, no one is limited, which I think is great.  

I truly believe The Lot will remain an impactful institution of New York and will continue to influence others into more creation and realize how easily things can come together with a great drive and strong community behind it. The Lot is special because it is so community oriented, it is forever growing because so they are open to everyone and their ideas and help to support them.
